text_content:
# MAIN ARTICLE


# One-Pot Synthesis of Algae-Like MoS2 /PPy Nanocomposite: A Synergistic Catalyst with Superior Peroxidase-Like Catalytic Activity for H2O2Detection


Junyu   Lei  ,   Xiaofeng   Lu  ,  *   Guangdi   Nie  ,   Ziqiao   Jiang  ,   and   Ce   Wang  *

A facile one-pot synthetic route is reported to prepare algae-like molybdenum disulfi  de/polypyrrole (MoS2   /PPy) nanocomposite through a redox reaction between ammonium tetrathiomolybdate and pyrrole monomer under a hydrothermal condition without any other templates. The as-prepared unique algae-like MoS2   /PPy nanocomposites are composed of few layer MoS2nanosheets, which are covered with PPy. Structural and morphological characterizations of this unique nanocomposite are investigated by Fouriertransform infrared spectra, Raman spectra, X-ray diffraction pattern, X-ray photoelectron spectra, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The as-prepared MoS2   /PPy nanocomposites exhibit an excellent peroxidase-like catalytic activity toward the oxidation of3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2 ) in acetate buffer solution (pH4.0), which provides a facile strategy for the colorimetric detection of H2O2with a high sensitivity.


# 1.   Introduction


Over  the  past10years,  a  great  number  of  reports  have  been focused  on  the  synthesis  and  applications  of  graphene-based nanomaterials because of their fascinating chemical and physical  properties.  Graphene  has  a2D  single  atomic  layer, providing  its  huge  surface  area,  strong  mechanical  strength, excellent  electron  mobility  and  thermal  conductivity,  which has  been  extensively  applied  in  the  fi  elds  of  optical  devices, [1] electronic devices, [2] electrochemical sources, [3] sensors, [4] catalysis, [5] etc.  Recently,  transition  metal  dichalcogenides  (TMDs) with  a2D  sheet-like  structure  have  attracted  more  and  more attention  owing  to  their  potential  applications  in  the  nextgeneration  electronics  and  optoelectronics.  Various  kinds  of2D TMD nanosheets such as molybdenum disulfi  de (MoS2 ), [6] NbS2 , [7] WS2 , [8] VS2 , [9] etc. with different chemical and physical properties  have  been  explored.  Among  these2D  TMD  nanomaterials,  MoS2is  a  naturally  occurring  molybdenite  compound,  building  up  from  three  atom  layers  (S-Mo-S)  stacked together  through  van  der  Waals  interactions. [10]   Comparing to the  indirect  bandgap  of1.2eV  in  bulk  MoS2 ,  few  layer  MoS2

J. Lei, Prof. X. Lu, G. Nie, Z. Jiang, Prof. C. Wang

Alan G. MacDiarmid Institute College of Chemistry Jilin University Changchun130012  ,   P. R. China E-mail:   xfl  u@jlu.edu.cn;   cwang@jlu.edu.cn

DOI: 10.1002/ppsc.201500043

has  a  direct  bandgap  of1.8eV  due  to the2D structure. [11] This unique feature brings few  layer MoS2signifi  cant improvement  in  optical,  electronic,  and mechanical  properties.  And  the  few  layer MoS2nanosheets have been attracted great interest  and  applications  in  catalysis, [12] phototransistors, [13] solar  cells, [14] lithium ion batteries, [15] electrocapacitor, [16] and fi  eld emission, [17] etc.

Polypyrrole (PPy) is one of the most frequently  studied  conducting  polymers  in the  past  few  decades  due  to  its  high  conductivity, high storage ability, good thermal and environmental stability, high redox and capacitive  current,  and  excellent  biocompatibility. [18]  Furthermore, the synthesis and purifi  cation of PPy are relatively easy. It has been  reported  that  PPy  and  its  derivatives could  be  synthesized  through  both  chem- ical  and  electrochemical  polymerization  approaches  in  either organic  or  aqueous  acid  media. [19] Recently,  PPy  nanoparticles have  been  reported  to  exhibit  intrinsic  peroxidase-like  activity, which could be employed for quantitative detection of H2O2generated  by  macrophages. [20] In  addition,  PPy  and  other  types  of conducting polymers have also be found to participate in the peroxidase-like reaction and enhance the catalytic performance. [21]

In this work, we for the fi  rst time prepared algae-like MoS2 / PPy nanocomposite in a one-pot reaction. The MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite  was  prepared  using  a  hydrothermal  route  to  have direct  oxidation  polymerization  of  pyrrole  in  the  presence  of ammonium  tetrathiomolybdate,  which  of  the  obtained  PPy could  in  situ  well-distributed  grown  on  the  surface  of  MoS2sheets. We also show the peroxidase-like activity of MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite  toward  the  oxidation  of  peroxidase  substrate3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine  (TMB)  in  the  presence  of  H2O2to  produce  a  blue  color.  In  addition,  a  comparative  catalytic activity has been made with that of the independent MoS2and PPy nanomaterial, which revealed that the synergistic coupling effect between few layer MoS2nanosheets and PPy component contributed the enhanced catalytic activities.


# 2.   Result and Discussion


- 2.1.   The Morphology and Structure of MoS2   /PPy Nanocomposite

As  shown  in Figure1A,B,  the  morphologies  of  MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite  were  characterized  by  transmission  electron

Figure1. A,B) TEM images of algae-like MoS2   /PPy nanostructure at different magnifi  cations. C) HRTEM image of the as-prepared MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite. D) EDX mapping images of MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite.

microscope  (TEM)  images.  It  is  obviously  shown  that  the obtained MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite displayed an algae-like morphology with a diameter of about several hundred nanometers, which is composed of the several folded thin pieces. And each slice  of  the  nanocomposite  has  a  few  layer  nanosheet  structure. This unique architecture of MoS2  /PPy composites should be attributed to the few layer MoS2self-assembling during the hydrothermal  process,  in  which  ammonium  tetrathiomolybdate was reduced to MoS2in the presence of pyrrole monomer and the obtained thin MoS2 /PPy composite self-assembled into a3D  architecture  by  partial  overlapping.  The  reaction  might undergo a redox reaction with the following formula: [22]

During the reaction process, hexavalent molybdenum as an oxidant  changed  to  quadrivalent  molybdenum,  while  pyrrole undergoes  an  in  situ  chemical  oxidization  polymerization  to generate PPy growing on the MoS2sheets.  For  a  comparison, the  morphology  of  the  pure  MoS2nanostructure  that  is  produced  by  hydrazine  as  the  reducing  agent  instead  of  pyrrole monomer  is  a  bit  different.  The  pure  MoS2nanostructures have  a  fl  ower-like  shape  with  a  sharper  edge  and  a  smaller diameter of the sheet assembly comparing to that of MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite (Figure S1, Supporting Information). From the high-resolution  transmission  electron  microscope  (HRTEM) image in Figure1C, the lattice fringes of MoS2 /PPy nanosheets are marked clearly. The fringe with a lattice spacing of0.27nm corresponds  to  the  (100)  plane  of  MoS2 .  The  (002)  plane  of MoS2with a lattice spacing of0.61nm is also observed on the Figure1C,  and  it  also  shows  some  possessing  folded  edges exhibiting  parallel  lines  corresponding  to  the  layers  of  MoS2sheets. [23] To  prove  the  formation  of  MoS2and  PPy  in  the nanocomposites,  energy-dispersive  X-ray  spectroscopy  (EDX) was performed. It can be seen clearly from the EDX mapping images (Figure1D) that the elements of N existed in the PPy/ MoS2nanocomposites  with  the  identical  shape  of  Mo  and  S, which showed the existence of PPy and MoS2in  the  product. EDX analysis of the MoS2  /PPy nanocomposites ( Figure2A) can also prove the existence of the C, N, S, Mo elements. These data proved that the PPy had been successfully generated along with the MoS2nanosheets via a one-pot synthetic strategy through a hydrothermal reaction.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of as-prepared MoS2 /PPy nanocomposites is shown in Figure2B. The broad diffraction peaks at14, 33, 57 ° represent the distinct (002), (100), and (110) diffraction peaks of MoS2 , indicating that the formed MoS2has a hexagonal structure (JCPDS77-1716). The primary (002) diffraction  peak  has  a  relative  low  intensity,  indicating  that  each

Figure2. A)  EDX  analysis  of  MoS2   /PPy  nanocomposite.  B)  XRD  pattern  of  MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite.

also verify the existence of MoS2 . In addition to  the  elements  of  Mo  and  S,  the  XPS  data also  displayed  the  existence  of  N  and  C  elements. As shown in Figure3C, the N1s spectrum  could  be  deconvoluted  into  two  components, with binding energies at400.6and399.9eV.  The  peak  at399.9eV  is  attributed to NH  in PPy, while the low intensity peak at400.6eV  is  related  to N state  in  PPy. The  high-resolution  C1s  spectrum  of  XPS shown  in  Figure3D  could  be  deconvoluted into three components, with binding energies at284.7, 285.4, and287.8eV. The component that appeared at284.7eV can be attributed to the  C C  group,  and  the  two  peaks  at285.4and287.8eV may be corresponding to C N

MoS2nanosheets in the MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite may contain only few layers, which are consistent with the conclusion determined by HRTEM image. Additionally, the XRD pattern of the MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite shows that the diffraction  peaks  of the PPy cannot be detected, indicating that the amorphous PPy was dispersed unevenly on the surface of MoS2sheets during the hydrothermal process.

X-ray  photoelectron  spectroscopy  (XPS)  was  used  to  investigate  the  chemical  states  of  Mo,  S,  and  N  in  the  MoS2 /PPy nanocomposites  ( Figure3 ).  The  peaks  of  binding  energies  at232.3and229.1eV shown in the high-resolution XPS spectrum is  attributed  to  Mo3d3/2and Mo3d5/2, respectively, which represent the Mo4 + existed in the composites. And S2p1/2and S2p3/2peaks are also observed at163.1and161.9eV, which and C N + bonds, respectively. The chemical states of N and C elements are consistent with that in PPy reported previously. [24] Comparing the XPS spectrum of the MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite with that of ammonium tetrathiomolybdate, the analysis of the chemical state of Mo, S, and N are quite different (Figure S2, Supporting  Information).  These  results  also  substantiate  the production of MoS2and PPy during the hydrothermal process.

The chemical composition of the resulting MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite  has  also  been  characterized  by  Fourier-transform infrared  (FTIR)  spectra. Figure4A shows the FTIR spectra of pure MoS2  , MoS2 /PPy, and MoS2 /PPy-2 (MoS2composite with PPy in double doze), respectively. Comparing with the spectrum of  the  pure  MoS2 ,  there  are  two  distinct  characteristic  bands of  PPy  at1543and1459cm -1in  the  spectrum  of  MoS2 /PPy,

Figure3. XPS spectra of the as synthesized MoS2   /PPy nanocomposite. A) Mo3d, B) S2p, C) N1s, D) C1s.

Figure4. A)  FTIR spectra of pure MoS2 ,  as  synthesized MoS2  /PPy  nanocomposite, and MoS2composite with PPy in double doze (MoS2 /PPy-2). B) Raman spectra of pure MoS2and MoS2   /PPy nanocomposite.

which could be attributed to the antisymmetric and symmetric pyrrole-ring fundamental vibration, respectively. To strengthen the  verifi  cation  of  the  existence  of  PPy,  nanocomposites  with PPy in double doze (MoS2 /PPy-2)  are  also  prepared  and  characterized, which the intensity of these above two characteristic bands fortify obviously along with the increase of the amount of  PPy,  further  proving  the  existence  of  PPy  in  the  nanocomposite after hydrothermal process. Raman spectrum is also an important  complementary  measurement  for  chemical  component  characterization.  Figure4B  shows  the  Raman  spectra of  both  MoS2nanofl  owers  and  the  as-synthesized  MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite.  Comparing  to  the  smooth  curve  of  the  MoS2spectra,  there  are  two  strong  peaks  at1333and1590cm -1in the spectrum of MoS2 /PPy, which are attributed to pyrrole-ring stretching  and  C C bond stretching, respectively. [25] All  these characterizations above certifi  cate the component and structure of the as-prepared MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite.


# 2.2.   Peroxidase-Like Catalytic Activity


In recent years, peroxidase-like catalysts for colorimetric detection of H2O2and other substances have attracted increasing attention of researchers and shown extensive potentials in practical application. [26]  It has been reported that TMDs such as CuS, [27] FeS, [28]

CdS, [29] and WS2 [30] are good candidates as the artifi  cial enzyme mimics. Recently, we have shown that Cu9S5nanocrystals decorated  on  the  surface  of  polyaniline  nanowires  could  exhibit  an enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity toward the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2compared to the individual PANI nanowires and pure Cu9S5nanocrystals. [  21d  ]  Here, we have verifi  ed  that  the  as-prepared  MoS2 /PPy  nanocomposite  displayed a  superior  peroxidase-like  activity  in  comparison  to  the  MoS2nanosheets  and  PPy  alone.  The  absorbance  changes  in  UVvis  spectrum  was  used  to  monitor  the  catalytic  process  during the  reaction.  It  is  well  known  that  pH  is  an  important  factor on the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction. As shown in Figure5 , the  MoS2 /PPy  nanocomposite  shows  a  pH-dependent  catalytic activity, and the maximum catalytic activity has been obtained at pH4.0. We further examined the effects of catalyst concentration and temperature on the catalytic activity of MoS2  /PPy nanocomposites toward the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 (Figure  S3,  Supporting Information). It was seen that the catalyst also showed temperatureand catalyst concentration-dependent catalytic activity.  The preferable catalytic activity was obtained under the optimal conditions of the temperature and catalyst concentration in the range of0-20 ° C and20-60 µg mL -1 , respectively. In this study, we took the condition of pH4.0, 20 ° C, and20 µg mL -1of catalyst to measure the peroxidase-like catalysis activity in the following experiments unless otherwise stated.

Figure5. A) The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the prepared MoS2   /PPy nanocomposites in acetate buffer solution with varied pH values. The inset in (A) is the photographs of TMB solutions oxidized in acetate buffer solution (pH4.0) recorded on10min of the catalytic reaction. B) The corresponding pH dependency of the catalytic relative activity of MoS2  /PPy nanocomposites. The maximum point in the curve was set as100%.

Figure6. The time-dependent absorbance changes at A) 651nm and B) the histogram with the addition of20 µg mL -1a) algae-like MoS2 /PPy nanocomposites, b) MoS2   /PPy-2, c) the mixture of PPy and MoS2nanofl  owers, d) pure MoS2nanofl  owers, e) PPy in the presence of100 × 10 -6M TMB and65 × 10 -3M H2O2 .

T o  discuss  the  peroxidase-like  activity  of  the  obtained  products,  the  UV-spectra  were  used  to  detect  the  time-dependent absorbance  changes  at651nm  with  the  addition  of  different kinds of catalysts in the presence of100 × 10 -6M of TMB and65 × 10 -3M of  H2O2 ( Figure6A).  By  comparison,  the  absorbance  response  of  individual  MoS2and  PPy  is  fi  rst  evaluated. The pure PPy has rarely increasing signal during the reactive process (curve e). The signal increased with time when using the individual MoS2as the catalyst (curve d), indicating the peroxidase-like  catalytic  reaction  was  in  progress  along  the  time. Although there are low peroxidase-like activity using these two independent  component,  the  algae-like  MoS2  /PPy  nanocomposites exhibited an excellent catalytic behavior toward the oxidation  of  the  colorimetric  substrate  TMB  by  H2O2 (curve  a), while the response of MoS2 /PPy-2 (curve b) has a bitter lower than that of MoS2  /PPy. The mechanism of enhanced catalytic activity may contribute to the synergistic effects between MoS2and PPy components. [  21d  ] Specifi  cally, the photogenerated electrons  excited  by  MoS2under  visible  light  irradiation  will transfer  from  MoS2sheets  to  the  covered  PPy,  which  effectively  prevent  the  recombination  of  the  electron-hole  pairs. And  H2O2is  a  good  electron  scavenger  to  form  OH·  on  the surface  of  nanocomposite,  which  largely  enhanced  the  oxidation  ability  of  TMB  ( Scheme1 ).  That  is  because  that  the  blue color product arose from the oxidation of TMB by OH·, which reported previously. [ 21d  ,  31  ] As  a  result,  the  algae-like  MoS2 /PPy nanocomposite  exhibited  an  enhanced  catalytic  activity  compared  to  the  individual  MoS2nanofl  owers  and  PPy  alone. This synergistic effect can be certifi  ed  by  another  comparison (Figure6A,  curve  c),  which  the  catalytic  effect  of  the  physical mixture  by  blending  the  two  components  together  (the  dose of  each  component  is  equal  to  the  other  catalysts  mentioned upon)  is  much  lower  than  that  of  MoS2 /PPy  nanocomposite. This unique phenomenon indicated that the catalytic effect of the physical mixture is only contribute to the single component of  MoS2and  PPy  in  the  blending  catalysts,  which  exhibited little  effect  in  electro  transfer.  The  histogram  also  shows  the outstanding  peroxidase-like  activity  of  the  obtained  MoS2 /PPy nanocomponent in these fi  ve catalyst (Figure6B).

The  peroxidase-like  behavior  of  MoS2 /PPy  was  discussed through  steady-state  kinetics  using  H2O2and  TMB  as  the independent  variables  separately.  Within  a  specifi  c  concentration scope of the substrate, the oxidation catalysis mediated by MoS2  /PPy nanocomposites conforms to the typical MichaelisMenten  model  (Figure  S4A,B,  Supporting  Information).  In both cases, the relationships between initial velocities and substrate  concentrations  can  be  fi  tted  to  double  reciprocal  plots with  unparallel  lines  almost  intersecting  at  a  point  left  of  the vertical  axis  (Figure  S4C,D,  Supporting  Information),  which is  consistent  with  a  sequential  mechanism  of  the  bisubstrate reaction. [32] The  kinetic  parameters,  including  the  Michaelis constant  ( Km  )  and  the  maximal  reaction  velocity  ( Vmax ),  were

Scheme1. A possible mechanism of the synergetic catalytic effect of the MoS2  /PPy nanocomposites toward the oxidation of TMB.

www.MaterialsViews.com

Figure7. A)  The  UV  absorbance  changes  in  the  presence  of  H2O2with  varied  concentrations  (intensity  from  strong  to  weak:  10 × 10 -3M  , 5 × 10 -3M  , 2 × 10 -3M  , 1 × 10 -3M  , 500 × 10 -6M  , 200 × 10 -6M  , 100 × 10 -6M  , 50 × 10 -6M  , 20 × 10 -6M  , 10 × 10 -6M  , 5 × 10 -6M, 2 × 10 -6M  , 1 × 10 -6M  ) in acetate buffer solution (pH4.0) and B) a dose-response curve for the detection of H2O2using MoS2 /PPy as an artifi  cial peroxidase. The inset in (B) is the linear calibration plot for H2O2 .

reckoned  from  the  extrapolated  Lineweaver-Burk  plots.  The apparent Km values of MoS2  /PPy nanocomposites for the H2O2substrate  and  TMB  substrate  are  approximately12.8 × 10 -3M and0.41 × 10 -3M  ,  respectively,  which  are  comparable  to  that of  horseradish  peroxidase  (HRP)  and  some  nanomaterials (Table S1, Supporting Information). [33]

Based  on  the  intrinsic  peroxidase-like  property  of  MoS2 / PPy  nanocomposite,  a  colorimetric  method  for  the  detection of  H2O2using  the  MoS2 /PPy  nanocomposite-catalyzed  blue color  reaction  is  realized.  Since  the  catalytic  activity  of  MoS2 / PPy nanocomposite is H2O2concentration-dependent, this can be used to detect H2O2by monitoring the absorbance changes at651nm  in  UV-vis  spectrum. Figure7A  shows  a  typical H2O2concentration-response  curve  in acetate buffer solution (pH4.0), revealing that the intensity of the absorbance at651nm  increases  with  the  increased  concentration  of  H2O2 . From  the  corresponding  calibration  plot  in  Figure7B,  it  has been found that H2O2could be well detected in the linear range from50 × 10 -6M  to2 × 10 -3M  ,  and  the  detection  limit  was estimated to be about45 × 10 -6M ( S / N = 3).


# 3.   Conclusion


In  summary,  the  unique  algae-like  MoS2 /PPy  nanostructure was prepared in a one-pot synthesis under a hydrothermal process through the redox reaction between ammonium tetrathiomolybdate and pyrrole monomer for the fi  rst time. The obtained MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite was fully characterized and then used as  a  catalyst  for  peroxidase-like  reaction.  The  results  exhibited that  an  enhanced catalytic performance compared with that of pure MoS2nanofl  owers, pure PPy, and their physical mixtures has  been  achieved,  which  might  arise  from  the  unique  algae structure compacted with sheets as well as the synergistic effects between MoS2and the PPy. Our fi  ndings may stimulate further studies  of  nanocomposites  with  TMDs  and  conducting  polymers as superior enzyme mimetics in the fi  eld of nanocatalysis.


# 4.   Experimental Section


Materials :  Pyrrole  monomer  was  obtained  from  Aladdin  Chemical Reagent Company. Ammonium tetrathiomolybdate was purchased from

Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH. All the aqueous solutions or dispersions were prepared using distilled water. All the other chemical reagents were in analytical grade and used without further purifi  cation.

Synthesis of Algae-Like MoS2   /PPy Nanocomposite : The algae-like MoS2 / PPy  nanocomposite  was  synthesized  as  follows:  20.5mg  ammonium tetrathiomolybdate was fi  rstly  dissolved  in20mL  distilled  water  under stirring. After stirring for about10min, 20 µL of pyrrole monomer was added  into  the  above  solution  and  stirred  for  another10min.  Then the mixed solution was transferred into a40mL Tefl  on-Lined stainless steel  autoclave  and  heated  at200 ° C  for15h.  After  cooled  to  room temperature, the resulting black precipitates of MoS2 /PPy were collected by  centrifugation  at10  000r  min -1 .  The  obtained  product  was  then washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times to remove the residue or reactants, and dried at40 ° C for24h under vacuum to obtain a  black  powder  (MoS2 /PPy).  For  comparison,  the  amount  of  pyrrole monomer was changed to40 µL in the reaction to obtain a composition marked as MoS2  /PPy-2. Pure MoS2nanostructure was also synthetized by the reduction of MoS2in the presence of hydrazine. Polypyrrole was also  produced  with  oxidization  by  ammonium  peroxydisulfate  under  a hydrothermal process.

Characterization :  The  morphology and structure of the samples were characterized by TEM (JEOL JEM-1200EX) and HRTEM (FEI Tecnai G2F20)  operated  at100and200kV,  separately.  EDX  and  EDX  mapping images  were  also  characterized  by  HRTEM  (FEI  Tecnai  G2F20).  FTIR spectra of KBr powder-pressed pellets were recorded on a Bruker Vector22Spectrometer. Raman spectra (Raman,LabRAM HR  Evolution) were  further used  in chemical  composition  characterization.  X-ray diffraction  (XRD,  PANalytical  B.V.  Empyrean)  with  CuK α radiation  was applied to investigate the crystallographic structure of the as-fabricated products. The chemical composition of MoS2  /PPy nanocomposite was characterized by XPS (Thermo Scientifi  c ESCALAB250). The peroxidaselike  catalytic  activity  was  studied  by  UV-vis  spectra  performed  on Shimadzu UV-2501PC spectrometer and Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrometer.

Catalytic  Activity  Measurement :  In  a  typical  colorimetric  experiment, 20  µL  of  TMB  solution  (15 × 10 -3M  in  dimethylsulfoxide,  DMSO) as  peroxidase  substrate  and20  µL  of  H2O2   (30%)  were  together added into3mL of acetate buffer solution (pH4.0, 20 ° C)  in  a4mL spectrophotometer cell. After stirring to get a uniform solution, 20  µL  of  catalyst  suspension  (3mg  mL -1 )  was  injected  to  the  abovementioned mixture quickly. The catalytic activity measurements were  conducted  in  time  course  mode  by  monitoring  the  absorbance changes  at651nm.  Afterwards  the  sample  in  cuvette  was  positioned immediately  in  the  cell  holder  of  UV-vis  spectrophotometer  for  the steady-state kinetic measurements by monitoring the absorbance changes at651nm. Unless otherwise stated, steady-state kinetic assays were  carried  out  using20  µg  mL -1MoS2 /PPy  in  a  reaction  volume  of3.0mL acetate buffer solution (pH4.0, 20 ° C) with0.1 × 10 -3M TMB as

www.particle-journal.com substrate, and the H2O2concentration was65 × 10 -3M  . The MichaelisMenten constant was calculated using the Lineweaver-Burk plot: 1/ ν = Km  / Vmax  ·(1/[ S ] + 1/ Km  ),  where ν is  the initial velocity, Vmax  is the maximal reaction velocity, Km is the maximal reaction velocity, and [ S ] is the concentration of the substrate.

Figure S3. (A) The corresponding catalyst concentration dependency of the catalytic relative activity of MoS2/PPy nanocomposites. Experiments were carried out using varied catalyst concentration in3mL of acetate buffer solution containing0.1mM TMB and65mM H2O2at room temperature. (B) The corresponding temperature dependency of the catalytic relative activity of MoS2/PPy nanocomposites. Experiments were carried out using20 μg·mL -1catalyst concentration in3mL of acetate buffer solution containing0.1mM TMB and65mM H2O2at varied temperature. The maximum point in each curve was set as100%.


# WILEY-VCH


Figure S4. Steady-state kinetic assay of MoS2/PPy nanocomposites. The catalyst concentration was fixed at20 μg·mL -1in3mL of acetate buffer solution (0.1M, pH = 4.0). (A) TMB concentration was kept constant at100 μM and the H2O2concentration was varied. (B) H2O2concentration was maintained at65mM and the TMB concentration was varied. Double reciprocal plots of MoS2/PPy nanocomposites catalytic activity for the two substrates (C) H2O2and (D) TMB. Details were described in the Experimental section.
